steps.title: Track the conclusion
steps.content: Persad and colleagues concluded that concentrating precipitation into fewer events would increase irrigation, but that the size of the increase would depend strongly on the area's baseline precipitation concentration. Evidence for that conclusion must compare irrigation output across the two baseline scenarios, not simply mention aquifer recharge or one isolated condition.

steps.title: Compare the irrigation data
steps.content: Choice A mixes irrigation data with aquifer data, so it misses the conclusion's focus. Choice C also includes aquifer data and only one baseline scenario. Choice D invents a decline in irrigation that the table does not show. The relevant contrast is that irrigation rises only slightly when baseline precipitation is somewhat concentrated, but rises 9.0% for surface water and 7.9% for groundwater when baseline precipitation is evenly distributed. The answer is B.
